sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Airway clearance support may assist selected stable patients with secretion burden, but it should never delay escalation in acute respiratory distress or unstable chest conditions.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Productive cough or difficulty clearing sputum
- Reduced breath efficiency with retained secretions
- Chronic respiratory disease with mucus burden
- Fatigue or weakness limiting effective cough

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Acute hypoxia, severe work of breathing, haemoptysis, chest trauma, or pneumothorax concern require medical management first
- Not every respiratory patient benefits from active airway clearance at the time of presentation
- Hydration, positioning, and underlying disease treatment often matter more than complex techniques in acute care

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen respiratory stability, SpO2, distress, and contraindications before trying airway-clearance support
- Use upright positioning, supported cough, and simple breathing control where appropriate
- Escalate if the patient is tiring, hypoxic, or unable to protect the airway

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Select simple secretion-mobilisation strategies that match the patient's stability and capacity
- Coordinate with medical therapy such as bronchodilators, humidification, or infection management rather than treating airway clearance in isolation
- Refer or escalate when chronic mucus burden, recurrent infection, or poor cough effectiveness suggests broader respiratory support needs

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Airway clearance is most useful when it improves real ventilation and secretion movement, not when it simply adds workload to a breathless patient
